original_abstract: Grounded in the Reformed theological principle of *Sola Scriptura*, this article examines the current crisis within youth ministry, specifically its struggle to cultivate lasting Christian maturity in young people. A central driver of this inefficiency is the increasing isolation of the youth from the adult world, particularly from their own parents and the broader congregational community. To address this, the author proposes a family-oriented model of youth ministry that emphasizes the primary responsibility of parents in their children's spiritual formation. Within this framework, the church’s role shifts toward equipping and supporting families in their discipleship journey. By identifying key stakeholders and reimagining their respective roles, this study offers fresh perspectives on fostering a more integrated and effective approach to ministry.
humanized_abstract: Youth Ministry: A Call to Re-Think This article makes a call to action in re-thinking the way youth ministry is done in our churches. Using the theology of Sola Scriptura that underpins Reformed theology, the article attempts to analyse and expose the inefficiency of the present systems of youth ministry and its inability to nurture genuine Christian growth in young people. The article concludes by pleading for a family-based model of youth ministry, where parents are the primary disciplers of their children and the church plays a supporting role in empowering and equipping families for disciple-making ministry. Identifying different actors in the field of youth ministry and by rethinking their functions and roles, this study looks at ways in which we can rethink and revitalise our approach to youth ministry.
